title: Tag Navigation
subtitle: Jump to definitions across files using a tags index.
summary: ctags builds a tags file mapping symbols to file:line. Ctrl-] jumps to the symbol under the cursor; {key:Ctrl-T} pops back. The whole jump history forms a stack you can browse with `:tags`.

blocks.text: Long before LSP, Vim had a 'go to definition.' It still works — and on big codebases it's faster than any language server because it's just a binary-searchable text index.
Run a tags generator (universal-ctags is the canonical one) at the root of your project:

rows: Recursively index everything under . into a file called `tags`

blocks.text: Vim looks for the `tags` file in the current directory, then walks parents. Configure with `:set` {key:t}{key:a}{key:g}{key:s}{key:=}{key:.}{key:/}{key:t}{key:a}{key:g}{key:s}{key:;}{key:/}{key:t}{key:a}{key:g}{key:s} to search both adjacent and root tags files.

sequence.note: Jump to the definition of the identifier under the cursor
Pop back to where you came from (one level)
Show menu of all matching tags (when the name is ambiguous)
Open the tag in a new horizontal split
Show the tag's definition in a preview window without leaving
Show the full tag stack with current position marked
Next match when the tag was ambiguous

blocks.title: Tags is a binary-searched text file
blocks.text: A `tags` file is plain text, one tag per line: `NAME<TAB>FILE<TAB>SEARCH_PATTERN`. The lines are sorted by NAME so the editor can find a tag with a binary search instead of a scan — which is why a 100MB tags file lookup is essentially instant. The SEARCH_PATTERN is usually a `/^...$/` regex that re-finds the line in the target file, so tag jumps survive small edits to the source between regenerations.

blocks.title: The tag stack is a real stack
blocks.text: {key:Ctrl-]} pushes `(buffer, position, tag)` onto the per-window tag stack and jumps. {key:Ctrl-T} pops. `:tags` prints the whole stack. Unlike the jump list (a ring buffer with duplicate suppression), the tag stack is a true LIFO — go down ten levels into a definition trail, hit {key:Ctrl-T} ten times to retrace exactly. Each window has its own stack, which is why splitting via {key:Ctrl-W}{key:]} starts a fresh trail in the new window.
Pair tags with the quickfix list (`:tselect` fills it with all matches) for cross-file refactor sweeps.
